impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products must have substantial non-infringing uses to escape antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the district court's determination that whether the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question, and the jury found against Ingevity. This case reinforces that patent holders cannot leverage valid patents to foreclose competition in separate product markets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF benefit from clear precedent that patent holders cannot bundle patent licenses with exclusive purchasing requirements for unpatented products, opening markets for competing suppliers.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust risk when structuring license agreements that require customers to purchase the licensor's unpatented products, even where the products are related to the patented technology.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on enforcement of fraudulently obtained patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both Tenaris's inequitable conduct in obtaining coiled tubing patents and Global Tubing's Walker Process antitrust fraud claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The court remanded for further proceedings, holding that the district court improperly resolved contested factual questions at summary judgment regarding whether Tenaris concealed prior art (the CYMAX Documents) during patent prosecution.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from the court's recognition that such determinations are fact-intensive and difficult to resolve at summary judgment. Patent challengers benefit from the court keeping Walker Process claims alive.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face continued litigation exposure where prior art known to the applicant is not disclosed during prosecution, as both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust claims may survive summary judgment.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to records of judicial proceedings and that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements rather than applying a blanket designation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic producers benefit from greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, potentially enabling better access to information needed for competition and compliance.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk that their business information may become publicly accessible, as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all submissions.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Office of Personnel Management's regulation requiring written authorization for overtime under 5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c) is a valid exercise of OPM's rulemaking authority under 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a), which requires payment for overtime 'officially ordered or approved.'
impacts.description: Sitting en banc, the Federal Circuit held that OPM's longstanding regulatory requirement that overtime be authorized in writing is a valid exercise of delegated rulemaking authority under the Federal Employees Pay Act. The court affirmed dismissal of Count II of the complaint, which sought overtime pay without written authorization. This ruling, rooted in 80 years of regulatory history, maintains the writing requirement as a prerequisite for federal employee overtime compensation.
impacts.positiveImplications: The federal government benefits from continued enforceability of the writing requirement for overtime authorization, providing clear administrative controls over overtime costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees who perform overtime work without written pre-authorization cannot recover overtime pay under 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a), even if supervisors were aware of and benefited from the additional work.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether IHS registered nurses hired under Title 5 but paid under Title 38's special salary provisions are entitled to additional Title 38 compensation enhancements for overtime, nighttime, holiday, and Sunday work.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit panel reversed the Court of Federal Claims' dismissal of claims by IHS registered nurses seeking enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38 provisions applicable to Title 5 healthcare employees. The court found the nurses adequately stated claims that IHS was required to apply these Title 38 pay enhancements and remanded for further proceedings. This ruling could affect compensation for thousands of federal healthcare workers.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies, particularly IHS, face potential liability for back pay and ongoing increased compensation costs for nurses and potentially other healthcare employees who may be entitled to Title 38 pay enhancements beyond base salary.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server tailoring content delivery specifications to match a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's judgment in favor of GoTV, holding that all asserted claims of the three patents-in-suit were ineligible for patenting under § 101. The claims, directed to server-side tailoring of content specifications to match device capabilities for streaming, were found to be directed to abstract ideas without inventive concepts. This reversal eliminated a $2.5 million jury verdict and resulted in judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Netflix benefit from continued narrowing of patent-eligible subject matter for software-implemented content delivery systems, reducing exposure to patent infringement suits based on abstract functional claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to server-side content adaptation for different devices face heightened invalidity risk under § 101, even after surviving district court challenges and obtaining favorable jury verdicts.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary decisions to decline institution of inter partes review proceedings constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing Board discretion to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the non-institution authority is insulated from judicial review, and the instructions do not bind the Director. This preserves the PTO's flexibility to guide IPR institution decisions through informal guidance.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's ability to maintain and update discretionary non-institution policies without formal rulemaking, which may result in fewer IPR challenges to their patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies like Apple that frequently petition for IPR face continued risk that informal PTO policy changes can restrict access to IPR proceedings without public input through notice-and-comment rulemaking.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy in a patent infringement case involving Azure cloud technology. The court found the district court applied an overly strict standard in excluding the damages expert's opinions, improperly denying Exafer the opportunity to prove damages. This decision reinforces that Daubert exclusions should not be used to effectively deny patent holders any remedy.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from greater latitude in presenting damages theories and expert testimony, reducing the risk that technical deficiencies in expert reports lead to dispositive exclusion rather than corrections through cross-examination.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defendants like Microsoft face reduced ability to obtain summary judgment through Daubert challenges to damages experts, potentially increasing patent litigation costs and settlement pressure.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of patent infringement claims against Google, holding that web conferencing patent claims directed to time-shifting capabilities and asynchronous playback of presentation data streams were ineligible under § 101. This continues the trend of invalidating software patents directed to functional improvements in content delivery and presentation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from continued § 101 protections against patent assertions based on functional claims for common web conferencing and streaming features.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering web conferencing and multimedia streaming functionalities face heightened invalidity risk at the motion to dismiss stage under § 101.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board's construction of the term 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content was correct, and whether the Board erred in rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge based on that construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's patent on streaming partly encrypted media content. The court adopted a different construction of 'encryption information' and vacated the Board's decision upholding patentability, remanding for the Board to reconsider Netflix's obviousness challenge under the corrected claim construction. This impacts the scope of DRM-related patents in the streaming industry.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies like Netflix benefit from a narrower construction of encryption-related patent claims, potentially enabling successful obviousness challenges and reducing patent exposure in content delivery systems.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the DRM and content protection space face risk of claim narrowing through appellate claim construction that may expose patents to prior art challenges previously deemed insufficient.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a money-mandating obligation entitling eligible applicants to grant payments under the Tucker Act, and whether the SBA's failure to award grants in the order applications were received states a compensable claim.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses stated viable Tucker Act claims against the United States for unpaid Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants. The court held that the RRF statute's mandatory language directing the SBA to 'award grants to eligible entities in the order in which applications are received' created enforceable rights, and the SBA's failure to follow the statutory order gave rise to potential damages claims. This ruling opens the door to significant government liability for pandemic relief program administration.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses and restaurants that applied for but did not receive RRF grants may have viable damages claims against the government, potentially recovering amounts they were statutorily entitled to receive.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potentially substantial liability for administrative failures in distributing pandemic relief funds, which may influence how future emergency grant programs are structured and administered.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development S.R.O. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Goodyear was entitled to judgment as a matter of law of no trade secret misappropriation after a jury awarded $64 million in compensatory and punitive damages for misappropriation of self-inflating tire technology trade secrets.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's grant of JMOL in favor of Goodyear, overturning a $64 million jury verdict ($2.8M compensatory, $61.2M punitive) for trade secret misappropriation of self-inflating tire technology. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship of Goodyear's related patent. This decision demonstrates the high evidentiary bar for trade secret claims even after securing a favorable jury verdict.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies that develop technology independently benefit from strong JMOL standards that can overturn even large jury verdicts for trade secret misappropriation where the evidence of actual use or derivation is insufficient.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret holders face significant risk that even favorable jury verdicts may be overturned at the JMOL stage, underscoring the need for strong direct evidence linking the defendant's products to misappropriated trade secrets.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Circuit has appellate jurisdiction over a non-final bond order imposed under the Idaho Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act, and whether the Act is preempted by federal patent law.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed appeals challenging an $8 million bond imposed under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act against patent assertion entities that sued Micron for infringement of expired semiconductor patents. The court held it lacked jurisdiction over the non-final bond order. The underlying case tests whether state anti-patent-troll legislation can impose bonds and liability on patent holders, with significant implications for patent enforcement strategies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Operating companies like Micron benefit from state anti-patent-troll statutes that impose financial burdens on bad-faith patent asserters, potentially deterring abusive patent litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face growing risk from state-level anti-patent-troll legislation that may require substantial bonds before pursuing infringement claims, increasing the cost and risk of patent enforcement.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether common law trademark rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement can establish priority for purposes of cancelling a prior registration and opposing intent-to-use applications under Section 2(d) of the Lanham Act.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' trademark registration and dismissal of Game Plan's opposition to UNIP's marks. The court held that UNIP validly acquired common law priority rights in 'MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' through an asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill, establishing priority over Game Plan's registration. This decision clarifies that acquired common law rights can defeat existing registrations in TTAB proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies that acquire trademark rights through asset purchases benefit from clear precedent that properly transferred common law rights, including goodwill, can establish priority over existing registrations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants face risk that competitors may acquire pre-existing common law rights through asset purchases and use them to cancel registrations, even after the registration has been in effect for years.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB erred in refusing registration of KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops as generic or merely descriptive under the doctrine of foreign equivalents based on the mark's meaning as a type of Kashmiri green tea.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops. The court found the Board's genericness and descriptiveness findings based on the Kashmiri green tea meaning of KAHWA were not supported by substantial evidence, as there was insufficient evidence that American consumers would associate the term with tea sold in cafés. This decision limits the reach of the doctrine of foreign equivalents and clarifies evidentiary standards for genericness determinations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses seeking to register foreign-language marks benefit from the court's requirement of substantial evidence that American consumers understand the foreign meaning, limiting over-broad application of the doctrine of foreign equivalents.
